feedback.text: Thank you for launching this initiative, These 4 points are important for general consumer well being. I personally have stopped playing games because of their aggressive push towards gambling. To many games are promoting or incentivizing gambling to teenagers or children. I also remember some executives trying to do in-game targeting to try and get you to buy loot boxes and micro-transactions in the most scummy way possible. They would match you against people with better weapons then you that you could only get from loot boxes so you'd spend money on them. And then when you did they would match you against people with worse weapons so you'd win and felt good about getting the loot boxes. They would keep this going in a cycle. All these 4 points Dark patterns, Addictive design, Making it impossible to unsubscribe from services (not just for games) and Paying "influencers" to promote predatory products to children and teens are bad for consumers and benefit nobody but the bottom line of these companies. I however am here to ask for a little bit of extra, maybe a 5th point. To stop killing games, In the same vain it is only fair that when you buy something, you own it. This Digital Fairness Act could also include some rules to make game manufacture companies stop willfully design games in such a way that they become unplayable on a whim or when the developer feels like it. Games cost a fair bit of money ranging from 40 up to 100 or more for some editions. Yet these games are designed in such a way that you would need to connect to their servers and if they turn off those servers the games become unplayable. This is unacceptable and cannot be though possible for any other product, yet this is totally normal in the gaming industry. Hundreds of games and soon hundreds more will become totally unplayable and inaccessible for the people that purchased the product. Its impossible to imagine you buy a car and in 3 years you cant drive it anymore because the company either doesn't feel like it or goes bankrupt. this is an extra point that could very well be added to a more Fair internet and more Fair consumer rights. Thank you for all the work everyone is doing on this initiative and i wish everyone the best. Consumer rights are important to everyone.
